examples.explanation: Start: dfs(0, [0,0], 0). Item0 (v=1,w=4): Try skip → dfs(1,[0,0],0). Try knapsack0 (capacity7): fits → dfs(1,[4,0],1). Try knapsack1 (capacity8): fits → dfs(1,[0,4],1). From dfs(1,[0,0],0): Item1 (v=2,w=5): Skip → dfs(2,[0,0],0). Knapsack0: fits → dfs(2,[5,0],2). Knapsack1: fits → dfs(2,[0,5],2). From dfs(1,[4,0],1): Item1: Skip → dfs(2,[4,0],1). Knapsack0: doesn't fit (4+5>7). Knapsack1: fits → dfs(2,[4,5],3). From dfs(1,[0,4],1): Item1: Skip → dfs(2,[0,4],1). Knapsack0: fits → dfs(2,[5,4],3). Knapsack1: doesn't fit (4+5>8). Continue all branches. At leaves: Max value found is 5 (items 1+2 in knapsack0+1 or items 0+2).

Start dfs(0,[0,0],0). Item0 (10,1): Skip → dfs(1,[0,0],0); Knapsack0 → dfs(1,[1,0],10); Knapsack1 → dfs(1,[0,1],10). From dfs(1,[0,0],0): Item1 (20,2): Skip → dfs(2,[0,0],0); Knapsack0 → dfs(2,[2,0],20); Knapsack1 → dfs(2,[0,2],20). From dfs(1,[1,0],10): Item1: Skip → dfs(2,[1,0],10); Knapsack0: fits → dfs(2,[3,0],30); Knapsack1: fits → dfs(2,[1,2],30). Continue exploring all combinations. Best combination: Put all items: item0 in knapsack0 (weight1), item1 in knapsack0 (weight2, total3), item2 in knapsack1 (weight3). Total value = 10+20+30 = 60.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible subsets of items and assign them to knapsacks, checking constraints like weight and capacity. Use recursion to explore every combination, but it's impractical for large inputs due to exponential growth.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^n * m) where n is number of items and m is number of knapsacks
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) for recursion stack and auxiliary storage

ladders.title: Full Problem — Multiple Knapsack with Constraints via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete brute force solution exploring all item subsets across multiple knapsacks with weight constraints.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force recursion approach for the multiple knapsack problem. The intuition is to systematically explore every possible way to assign items to knapsacks by generating all subsets of items and checking if they can be placed in the available knapsacks while respecting weight constraints. We use depth-first recursion where at each step we decide whether to place the current item in each knapsack (if it fits) or skip it entirely. The algorithm maintains current weights in each knapsack and tracks the maximum total value found. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize global max_value = 0. 2. Define recursive function dfs(index, current_weights, current_value). 3. Base case: if index == n (all items processed), update max_value if current_value > max_value. 4. Recursive case: For current item at index, try: a) Skip item: dfs(index+1, current_weights, current_value). b) For each knapsack i where item fits (current_weights[i] + weight[index] ≤ capacity[i]): Add item to knapsack i: dfs(index+1, updated_weights, current_value + value[index]). 5. Start recursion with dfs(0, [0]*m, 0). Time complexity is O(2^n * m) because we explore 2^n subsets and for each we try up to m knapsacks. Space complexity is O(n + m) for recursion stack and weight arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive exploration from L1 as its core engine. Common mistakes include not properly backtracking weight changes (must restore state after recursive calls) and not considering that items can be skipped. Real code pattern: def solve(values, weights, capacities): n, m = len(values), len(capacities); max_val = 0; def dfs(i, curr_weights, curr_val): nonlocal max_val; if i == n: max_val = max(max_val, curr_val); return; dfs(i+1, curr_weights, curr_val); for k in range(m): if curr_weights[k] + weights[i] <= capacities[k]: curr_weights[k] += weights[i]; dfs(i+1, curr_weights, curr_val + values[i]); curr_weights[k] -= weights[i]; dfs(0, [0]*m, 0); return max_val.

examples.trace: Start: dfs(0, [0,0], 0). Item0 (v=1,w=4): Try skip → dfs(1,[0,0],0). Try knapsack0 (capacity7): fits → dfs(1,[4,0],1). Try knapsack1 (capacity8): fits → dfs(1,[0,4],1). From dfs(1,[0,0],0): Item1 (v=2,w=5): Skip → dfs(2,[0,0],0). Knapsack0: fits → dfs(2,[5,0],2). Knapsack1: fits → dfs(2,[0,5],2). From dfs(1,[4,0],1): Item1: Skip → dfs(2,[4,0],1). Knapsack0: doesn't fit (4+5>7). Knapsack1: fits → dfs(2,[4,5],3). From dfs(1,[0,4],1): Item1: Skip → dfs(2,[0,4],1). Knapsack0: fits → dfs(2,[5,4],3). Knapsack1: doesn't fit (4+5>8). Continue all branches. At leaves: Max value found is 5 (items 1+2 in knapsack0+1 or items 0+2).

Start dfs(0,[0,0],0). Item0 (10,1): Skip → dfs(1,[0,0],0); Knapsack0 → dfs(1,[1,0],10); Knapsack1 → dfs(1,[0,1],10). From dfs(1,[0,0],0): Item1 (20,2): Skip → dfs(2,[0,0],0); Knapsack0 → dfs(2,[2,0],20); Knapsack1 → dfs(2,[0,2],20). From dfs(1,[1,0],10): Item1: Skip → dfs(2,[1,0],10); Knapsack0: fits → dfs(2,[3,0],30); Knapsack1: fits → dfs(2,[1,2],30). Continue exploring all combinations. Best combination: Put all items: item0 in knapsack0 (weight1), item1 in knapsack0 (weight2, total3), item2 in knapsack1 (weight3). Total value = 10+20+30 = 60.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Subset Exploration with Knapsack Assignment
ladders.desc: Recursive function that explores item assignments to multiple knapsacks.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the subset generation and knapsack assignment logic. The intuition is to treat each item sequentially and make three types of decisions: skip the item, or assign it to any knapsack where it fits. This creates a decision tree where each path represents a complete assignment of items to knapsacks. The algorithm works by maintaining the current state (which items have been assigned, current weights in each knapsack, and current total value) and exploring all possible continuations. Step-by-step: 1. Function parameters: current item index, array of current weights in each knapsack, current total value. 2. Base case: If we've processed all items, record the solution if it's the best so far. 3. Recursive case: For current item, first try skipping it (recursive call with next index, same weights and value). Then, for each knapsack, if adding the item's weight doesn't exceed capacity, temporarily add it (update weights and value), make recursive call, then backtrack by removing it. Time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(m^depth) in worst case but when integrated with full problem becomes O(2^n * m). Space complexity is O(m + recursion depth). Connection to L0: This is the recursive engine that L0 calls to explore all possibilities. Connection to L2: This implements the branching logic described in L2. Common mistakes include forgetting to backtrack weight changes (leading to incorrect state propagation) and not considering the 'skip' option. Real code pattern: def explore(index, curr_weights, curr_val): if index == n: update_best(curr_val); return; explore(index+1, curr_weights, curr_val); for k in range(m): if curr_weights[k] + weights[index] <= capacities[k]: curr_weights[k] += weights[index]; explore(index+1, curr_weights, curr_val + values[index]); curr_weights[k] -= weights[index];.

examples.input: values = [5, 3], weights = [4, 2], capacities = [6], start at index=0 with curr_weights=[0], curr_val=0
examples.trace: Call explore(0,[0],0). Item0 (5,4): Skip → explore(1,[0],0). Add to knapsack0 → fits (0+4≤6) → explore(1,[4],5). From explore(1,[0],0): Item1 (3,2): Skip → explore(2,[0],0) → base case, value=0. Add to knapsack0 → fits → explore(2,[2],3) → base case, value=3. From explore(1,[4],5): Item1: Skip → explore(2,[4],5) → value=5. Add to knapsack0 → doesn't fit (4+2>6). Best value = max(0,3,5) = 5? Wait, actually we need to consider putting both items: That happens in path: Item0 added, then item1 skipped gives value 5; but putting both doesn't fit. Actually putting item0 skipped and item1 added gives value 3. But we can put item1 only or both? Both don't fit. So best is 5. But the problem says output 8? Let me recalc: values [5,3], weights [4,2], capacity 6. Both items weight 6, value 8, fits! So trace: From explore(0,[0],0): Add item0 → explore(1,[4],5). From there add item1 → fits (4+2=6) → explore(2,[6],8) → value 8. So best is 8.

examples.input: values = [2, 4], weights = [1, 3], capacities = [2, 2], start at index=0 with curr_weights=[0,0], curr_val=0
examples.trace: Call explore(0,[0,0],0). Item0 (2,1): Skip → explore(1,[0,0],0). Add to knapsack0 → fits → explore(1,[1,0],2). Add to knapsack1 → fits → explore(1,[0,1],2). From explore(1,[0,0],0): Item1 (4,3): Skip → explore(2,[0,0],0) → value 0. Add to knapsack0 → doesn't fit (0+3>2). Add to knapsack1 → doesn't fit (0+3>2). From explore(1,[1,0],2): Item1: Skip → explore(2,[1,0],2) → value 2. Add to knapsack0 → doesn't fit (1+3>2). Add to knapsack1 → fits → explore(2,[1,3],6) → value 6. From explore(1,[0,1],2): Item1: Skip → explore(2,[0,1],2) → value 2. Add to knapsack0 → fits → explore(2,[3,1],6) → but knapsack0 weight 3>2? Actually 0+3=3>2 doesn't fit. Add to knapsack1 → doesn't fit (1+3>2). Best value = 6.

values = [2, 3], weights = [1, 2], capacities = [2], start index=0, curr_weights=[0], curr_val=0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Branching Decision Tree for Item Assignment
ladders.desc: The branching logic of deciding for each item: skip or assign to a knapsack.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental decision-making process at each step of the recursion. The intuition is that for each item, we have (m+1) possibilities: we can skip the item entirely, or we can assign it to any of the m knapsacks where it fits. This creates a branching factor of up to (m+1) at each level of the recursion tree, leading to exponential exploration. The core insight is that by making these local decisions sequentially for all items, we eventually explore all possible global assignments. The algorithm conceptually: For item i, generate (m+1) child nodes: 1 child for 'skip', and m children for 'assign to knapsack k' (if weight fits). Each child node then recursively processes the next item. This builds a complete decision tree where leaves represent complete assignments. Time complexity arises from the branching: worst-case O((m+1)^n) but actually O(2^n * m) because not all branches are valid due to weight constraints. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This branching logic is what the recursive function in L1 implements. Connection to L3: This relies on the ability to check weight constraints (L3) at each branch. Common mistakes include not considering all knapsacks for assignment or incorrectly pruning branches. Real code pattern: At each recursive call for item i: # Option 1: Skip item; recurse with i+1. # Option 2: For each knapsack k from 0 to m-1: if current_weight[k] + weight[i] <= capacity[k]: assign item to knapsack k; recurse with i+1; unassign (backtrack).

examples.input: Item: value=5, weight=3. Current weights: knapsack0=2, knapsack1=4. Capacities: [5, 6]. m=2.
examples.output: Possible decisions: skip, assign to knapsack0, assign to knapsack1
examples.trace: For this item: Check skip: always possible. Check assign to knapsack0: current weight 2 + item weight 3 = 5 ≤ capacity 5 → fits, possible. Check assign to knapsack1: current weight 4 + 3 = 7 > capacity 6 → doesn't fit, not possible. So branching: 2 possible decisions (skip or assign to knapsack0).

examples.input: Item: value=10, weight=7. Current weights: [0, 0]. Capacities: [8, 5]. m=2.
examples.output: Possible decisions: skip, assign to knapsack0
examples.trace: Skip: possible. Assign to knapsack0: 0+7=7 ≤ 8 → fits. Assign to knapsack1: 0+7=7 > 5 → doesn't fit. So 2 branches.

testCases.expected: skip, assign to knapsack0, assign to knapsack1
skip, assign to knapsack0, assign to knapsack1
skip, assign to knapsack0, assign to knapsack1
skip, assign to knapsack0, assign to knapsack1

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Weight Constraints for Knapsack Assignment
ladders.desc: Determining if an item can be added to a knapsack based on current weight and capacity.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses the fundamental constraint-checking operation that's needed at each branching decision. The intuition is simple: before assigning an item to a knapsack, we must verify that adding the item's weight won't exceed that knapsack's capacity. This is a feasibility check that ensures we only explore valid assignments. The operation involves: 1. Knowing the current total weight in the knapsack. 2. Knowing the item's weight. 3. Knowing the knapsack's capacity. 4. Checking if current_weight + item_weight ≤ capacity. If true, the assignment is possible; if false, it's invalid and that branch shouldn't be explored. This check is performed for each knapsack at each decision point. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This constraint check is what determines which of the m assignment branches in L2 are valid. Connection to L4: This uses basic arithmetic comparison from L4. Common mistakes include using strict inequality (< instead of ≤) or not updating current weight correctly. Real code pattern: def can_assign(current_weight, item_weight, capacity): return current_weight + item_weight <= capacity.

examples.trace: Calculate: 4 + 3 = 7. Check: 7 ≤ 8 is true. So assignment is possible.
5 + 6 = 11. Check: 11 ≤ 10 is false. So assignment not possible.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Arithmetic Addition and Comparison
ladders.desc: Adding two numbers and comparing the sum to a threshold.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental mathematical operation required: adding the current weight and item weight, then comparing the result to the capacity. The intuition is that we need to perform basic arithmetic to determine feasibility. The operation consists of: 1. Addition: sum = a + b, where a is current weight, b is item weight. 2. Comparison: check if sum ≤ c, where c is capacity. This uses the less-than-or-equal operator. In programming, this is typically a single expression: a + b <= c. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L3: This is the exact computation performed in L3's constraint check. Connection to L5: This builds on understanding numbers and comparison operators from L5. Common mistakes include integer overflow (not relevant for small inputs) or incorrect operator precedence. Real code pattern: result = (current_weight + item_weight) <= capacity.

examples.trace: Add: 3 + 4 = 7. Compare: 7 ≤ 10 is true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Numbers and Comparison
ladders.desc: Basic knowledge of integers and comparison operators (≤).
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute prerequisite: understanding what numbers are and how to compare them. The intuition is that we need to work with numerical values (weights, capacities) and determine relationships between them. Key concepts: 1. Integers: whole numbers like 0, 1, 2, etc., representing weights and capacities. 2. Addition: combining two numbers to get a sum. 3. Comparison operators: specifically ≤ (less than or equal to), which checks if one number is less than or equal to another. For example, 5 ≤ 5 is true, 5 ≤ 10 is true, 10 ≤ 5 is false. This foundational knowledge allows us to express constraints like 'current weight plus item weight must not exceed capacity.' Time/space: not applicable conceptually. Connection to L4: This provides the semantic understanding for the operations in L4. Common mistakes include confusing < and ≤, or not understanding integer values. Real code pattern: In programming, we assume variables hold integer values and use <= operator.

examples.trace: 5 is less than 8, so 5 ≤ 8 is true.
7 is equal to 7, so 7 ≤ 7 is true.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(n * m * C)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] represents the maximum value achievable with first i items and j knapsacks, considering constraints like weight limits. Iterate over items and knapsacks to fill the table based on inclusion or exclusion decisions.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m * C) where C is total capacity across knapsacks
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n * m) for the DP table

ladders.title: Full Problem — Multiple Knapsack with Constraints using 2D DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution for multiple knapsack problem with weight constraints using 2D DP table.
ladders.explanation: This problem involves selecting items to maximize total value while respecting weight constraints across multiple knapsacks. The intuition is to extend the classic 0/1 knapsack DP approach to handle multiple knapsacks by tracking both items and knapsacks. We maintain a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] represents the maximum value achievable using the first i items and j knapsacks. For each item, we consider including it in each knapsack (if it fits) or excluding it. The recurrence relation is: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j-1] + value[i] if weight[i] <= capacity[j]). We iterate through items and knapsacks, ensuring we don't exceed capacities. Time complexity is O(n * m * C) where n is items, m is knapsacks, and C is total capacity. Space is O(n * m). Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of item inclusion/exclusion decisions. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing in nested loops and forgetting to handle cases where items don't fit. Real code pattern: Initialize dp with zeros, nested loops for items and knapsacks, update based on inclusion if weight <= capacity, else exclusion.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dp[4][3] (items+1, knapsacks+1) with zeros. Items: (weight,value) = (2,3), (3,4), (4,5). Knapsacks capacities: 5,6. Step 2: For i=1 (item1 weight2 value3): For j=1 (knapsack1 cap5): Include: dp[0][0]+3=3, exclude: dp[0][1]=0, max=3. For j=2: Include: dp[0][1]+3=3, exclude: dp[0][2]=0, max=3. Step 3: i=2 (item2 weight3 value4): j=1: Include if weight3<=5: dp[1][0]+4=4, exclude: dp[1][1]=3, max=4. j=2: Include: dp[1][1]+4=7, exclude: dp[1][2]=3, max=7. Step 4: i=3 (item3 weight4 value5): j=1: Include if weight4<=5: dp[2][0]+5=5, exclude: dp[2][1]=4, max=5. j=2: Include: dp[2][1]+5=9, exclude: dp[2][2]=7, max=9. Final dp[3][2]=9.

Step 1: dp[3][3] zeros. Items: (1,1), (2,3). Knapsacks: 2,3. Step 2: i=1 (1,1): j=1: Include: 0+1=1, exclude:0, max=1. j=2: Include: 0+1=1, exclude:0, max=1. Step 3: i=2 (2,3): j=1: Include if 2<=2: dp[1][0]+3=3, exclude: dp[1][1]=1, max=3. j=2: Include: dp[1][1]+3=4, exclude: dp[1][2]=1, max=4. Final dp[2][2]=4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Item Inclusion/Exclusion Decision for Multiple Knapsacks
ladders.desc: Decision logic for including or excluding an item across knapsacks in DP.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine determines whether to include an item in a knapsack or exclude it, considering weight constraints and maximizing value. The intuition is that for each item and knapsack, we compare two options: excluding the item (keeping value from previous items) or including it (adding its value if it fits). We use DP state transition: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j-1] + value[i] if weight[i] <= capacity[j]). This captures the optimal substructure property. Step-by-step: For current item i and knapsack j, check if item weight <= knapsack capacity. If yes, consider including: value from previous items with one less knapsack plus current item value. Always consider excluding: value from previous items with same knapsacks. Take maximum. Time O(1) per decision, space O(1). Connection to L0: This decision is applied in nested loops to build the full solution. Common mistakes: Incorrect capacity check or wrong DP indices. Real code pattern: if weight[i] <= capacity[j]: include_val = dp[i-1][j-1] + value[i]; exclude_val = dp[i-1][j]; dp[i][j] = max(include_val, exclude_val).

examples.trace: Step 1: Current state: dp_prev represents values with previous items for knapsacks 0,1,2. For knapsack_index=2, dp_prev[2]=5. Step 2: Check inclusion: weight4 <= capacity5? Yes. Include value = dp_prev[1] + 6 = 3 + 6 = 9. Step 3: Exclude value = dp_prev[2] = 5. Step 4: Max(9,5) = 9.
Step 1: dp_prev[1]=2, dp_prev[2]=4. Step 2: Check inclusion: weight3 <= capacity2? No. Cannot include. Step 3: Include value not considered. Exclude value = dp_prev[2] = 4. Step 4: Max is 4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — 2D DP State Transition for Multiple Knapsacks
ladders.desc: Defining and updating DP states for items and knapsacks combination.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves defining a 2D DP table where state dp[i][j] represents the maximum value achievable using the first i items and j knapsacks. The intuition is to break the problem into subproblems: for each additional item, decide how to allocate it across knapsacks. The recurrence builds on optimal solutions for fewer items or knapsacks. Step-by-step: Initialize dp[0][j] = 0 for all j (no items give zero value). For i from 1 to n (items), j from 1 to m (knapsacks): dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j-1] + value[i] if weight[i] <= capacity[j]). This ensures we consider all possibilities. Time O(n*m) for state updates, space O(n*m). Connection to L1: This uses the inclusion/exclusion decision repeatedly. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices or misordering loops. Real code pattern: dp = [[0]*(m+1) for _ in range(n+1)]; for i in range(1,n+1): for j in range(1,m+1): update as per recurrence.

ladders.title: Building Block — 0/1 Knapsack DP for Single Knapsack
ladders.desc: Dynamic programming solution for classic 0/1 knapsack with one knapsack.
ladders.explanation: This building block solves the foundational 0/1 knapsack problem: given items with weights and values, and a single knapsack capacity, maximize total value without exceeding weight. The intuition uses DP with a 1D array dp[w] representing max value for weight w. For each item, iterate capacities backwards to avoid overwriting. Recurrence: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight] + value). Step-by-step: Initialize dp[0..capacity]=0. For each item: for w from capacity down to item.weight: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-item.weight] + item.value). Time O(n*capacity), space O(capacity). Connection to L2: Extending this to multiple knapsacks requires adding a dimension for knapsacks. Common mistakes: Iterating capacities forwards causes multiple uses of same item. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(capacity+1); for weight, value in items: for w in range(capacity, weight-1, -1): dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight] + value).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Items and Capacities in DP
ladders.desc: Fundamental loop structure for DP updates in knapsack problems.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves the nested iteration over items and capacities (or knapsacks) to fill DP tables. The intuition is that DP requires systematic exploration of all possibilities: for each item, we consider all relevant capacities to update states. In knapsack DP, we typically have an outer loop for items and an inner loop for capacities, iterating backwards to ensure each item is used at most once. Step-by-step: Outer loop: for i in range(n): process item i. Inner loop: for w in range(capacity, weight[i]-1, -1): update dp[w]. This order prevents multiple uses of the same item. Time O(n*capacity) for the loops, space O(capacity). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the 0/1 knapsack DP. Common mistakes: Incorrect loop bounds or direction. Real code pattern: for item in items: for w in reversed(range(item.weight, capacity+1)): dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-item.weight] + item.value).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Weights, Values, and Capacity Constraints
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of items with weights and values, and knapsack capacity limits.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces the core elements of knapsack problems: items, weights, values, and capacity constraints. Each item has a weight (resource consumption) and value (benefit). A knapsack has a capacity (maximum total weight it can hold). The goal is to select items to maximize total value without exceeding capacity. Intuition: Think of packing a bag with limited space; you want the most valuable items that fit. Step-by-step: 1. List items with (weight, value). 2. Identify knapsack capacity. 3. Check if an item can be included: weight <= remaining capacity. 4. Calculate total value of selected items. Time O(1) for basic checks, space O(1). Connection to L4: This understanding is prerequisite for DP loops. Common mistakes: Confusing weight with value or misinterpreting capacity. Real code pattern: Represent items as tuples (weight, value); capacity as integer; check with if weight <= capacity.

examples.output: Can include both? Yes, total weight 5, value 25
examples.trace: Step 1: Item1 weight2 value10, Item2 weight3 value15. Step 2: Total weight if both included: 2+3=5. Step 3: Capacity=5, so 5<=5? Yes. Step 4: Total value = 10+15=25.

examples.output: Can include both? No, total weight 9 > 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Item1 weight4 value8, Item2 weight5 value9. Step 2: Total weight if both: 4+5=9. Step 3: Capacity=7, so 9<=7? No. Step 4: Cannot include both; must choose one or none.

testCases.expected: Can include both? No, total weight 4 > 3
Can include both? Yes, total weight 5, value 12
Can include both? No, total weight 2 > 1
Can include both? Yes, total weight 8, value 14

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Bitmask O(n * 2^m)
approaches.summary: Represent knapsack assignments as bitmasks to track which knapsacks are used for subsets of items, enabling efficient state transitions. This approach is useful when the number of knapsacks m is small, as it reduces the state space compared to 2D DP.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * 2^m) where m is number of knapsacks

ladders.title: Full Problem — Multiple Knapsack with Bitmask DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming with bitmask representation to assign items to multiple knapsacks.
ladders.explanation: The multiple knapsack problem requires distributing n items with given weights and values across m knapsacks with capacity constraints to maximize total value. When m is small (typically ≤ 20), we can use bitmask DP where each state dp[i][mask] represents the maximum value achievable using first i items with knapsack usage pattern represented by mask. Each bit in mask corresponds to a knapsack: 0 means unused, 1 means used. For each item, we try all possible assignments to knapsacks where the item fits, updating states accordingly. The intuition is that by tracking which knapsacks are used via bitmask, we avoid exponential blow-up in state space that would occur with traditional multi-dimensional DP. The algorithm initializes dp[0][0] = 0, then iterates through items and masks. For each state, we consider not taking the item (carry forward) or taking it by assigning to any knapsack where it fits, updating the mask to mark that knapsack as used. Time complexity is O(n * 2^m * m) due to iterating over items, masks, and knapsacks; space is O(n * 2^m). This connects to L1 by using the state transition logic as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include incorrect mask updates (need to check if knapsack bit is already set) and missing the 'not take' case. Real code pattern: dp = [[-inf]*(1<<m) for _ in range(n+1)]; dp[0][0]=0; for i in range(1,n+1): for mask in range(1<<m): dp[i][mask]=dp[i-1][mask]; for k in range(m): if not (mask>>k)&1 and weight[i-1]<=capacity[k]: new_mask=mask|(1<<k); dp[i][new_mask]=max(dp[i][new_mask], dp[i-1][mask]+value[i-1]); return max(dp[n][mask] over all masks).

examples.input: n=3 items: weights=[2,3,4], values=[5,7,9]; m=2 knapsacks: capacities=[5,5]
examples.trace: Initialize dp[0][0]=0. Item1 (w2,v5): from dp[0][0], not take→dp[1][0]=0; take in knap0→dp[1][1]=5; take in knap1→dp[1][2]=5. Item2 (w3,v7): from dp[1][0], not take→dp[2][0]=0; take in knap0→dp[2][1]=7; knap1→dp[2][2]=7. From dp[1][1] (mask01), not take→dp[2][1]=max(7,5)=7; take in knap1 (fits, bit1=0)→new_mask=01|10=11, dp[2][11]=5+7=12. From dp[1][2] (mask10), not take→dp[2][2]=max(7,5)=7; take in knap0→new_mask=10|01=11, dp[2][11]=max(12,5+7)=12. Item3 (w4,v9): from dp[2][0], not take→dp[3][0]=0; take in knap0→dp[3][1]=9; knap1→dp[3][2]=9. From dp[2][1] (mask01), not take→dp[3][1]=max(9,7)=9; take in knap1→new_mask=01|10=11, dp[3][11]=7+9=16. From dp[2][2] (mask10), not take→dp[3][2]=max(9,7)=9; take in knap0→new_mask=10|01=11, dp[3][11]=max(16,7+9)=16. From dp[2][11] (mask11), not take→dp[3][11]=max(16,12)=16; cannot take (both knaps used). Final max over masks: dp[3][0]=0, [1]=9, [2]=9, [3]=16 → 16.

examples.input: n=2 items: weights=[1,2], values=[10,20]; m=1 knapsacks: capacities=[2]
examples.trace: m=1 so masks: 0 (unused), 1 (used). Initialize dp[0][0]=0. Item1 (w1,v10): from dp[0][0], not take→dp[1][0]=0; take in knap0→dp[1][1]=10. Item2 (w2,v20): from dp[1][0], not take→dp[2][0]=0; take in knap0→dp[2][1]=20. From dp[1][1] (mask1), not take→dp[2][1]=max(20,10)=20; cannot take (knap used). Final max: dp[2][0]=0, [1]=20 → 20.

testCases.input: n=1 items: weights=[3], values=[5]; m=1 capacities=[2]
n=1 items: weights=[3], values=[5]; m=1 capacities=[5]
n=2 items: weights=[1,1], values=[2,3]; m=1 capacities=[1]
n=2 items: weights=[2,3], values=[3,4]; m=2 capacities=[3,3]
n=3 items: weights=[1,2,3], values=[6,10,15]; m=2 capacities=[3,3]
n=4 items: weights=[2,3,4,5], values=[3,4,5,6]; m=3 capacities=[5,5,5]
n=3 items: weights=[5,5,5], values=[10,10,10]; m=2 capacities=[10,10]
n=5 items: weights=[1,2,3,4,5], values=[1,2,3,4,5]; m=2 capacities=[7,7]
n=3 items: weights=[1,1,1], values=[1,2,3]; m=3 capacities=[1,1,1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — State Transition with Bitmask Updates
ladders.desc: The core state transition logic for assigning an item to knapsacks using bitmask representation.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the state transition in the DP: given current state (item index i, mask representing used knapsacks), we compute new states by either skipping the item or assigning it to an available knapsack where it fits. The mask is a bitmask of length m where bit k is 1 if knapsack k is used, 0 otherwise. For transition, we first carry forward the 'not take' case: dp[i][mask] = dp[i-1][mask]. Then for each knapsack k, if bit k in mask is 0 and item weight ≤ capacity[k], we create new_mask = mask | (1<<k) and update dp[i][new_mask] = max(dp[i][new_mask], dp[i-1][mask] + value[i]). The intuition is that each assignment decision is independent and we track used knapsacks via bitmask to avoid reusing them. Time complexity for this sub-routine per state is O(m) for checking knapsacks; space is O(1) per transition. This feeds into L0 as the inner loop of the full DP. Common mistakes include forgetting to check if knapsack bit is already set (leading to double usage) or not handling the 'not take' case. Real code pattern: for mask in range(1<<m): dp[i][mask] = dp[i-1][mask]; for k in range(m): if (mask>>k)&1 == 0 and weight[i-1] <= capacity[k]: new_mask = mask | (1<<k); dp[i][new_mask] = max(dp[i][new_mask], dp[i-1][mask] + value[i-1]).

examples.input: Current state: i=1, mask=0 (binary 00), dp[i-1][mask]=0; item: weight=2, value=5; m=2 capacities=[5,5]
examples.output: New states: mask=0 (not take) with value 0; mask=01 (use knap0) with value 5; mask=10 (use knap1) with value 5
examples.trace: Start with mask=00. Not take: dp[1][00] = dp[0][00] = 0. Check knap0: bit0=0, weight2≤5 → new_mask=00|01=01, dp[1][01] = max(-inf, 0+5)=5. Check knap1: bit1=0, weight2≤5 → new_mask=00|10=10, dp[1][10] = max(-inf, 0+5)=5. So states updated: (00,0), (01,5), (10,5).

examples.input: Current state: i=2, mask=01 (binary 01), dp[i-1][mask]=5; item: weight=3, value=7; m=2 capacities=[5,5]
examples.output: New states: mask=01 (not take) with value 5; mask=11 (use knap1) with value 12
examples.trace: Start with mask=01. Not take: dp[2][01] = dp[1][01] = 5. Check knap0: bit0=1 (set) → skip. Check knap1: bit1=0, weight3≤5 → new_mask=01|10=11, dp[2][11] = max(-inf, 5+7)=12. So states: (01,5), (11,12).

testCases.input: i=1, mask=0, dp_prev=0; weight=1, value=10; m=1 capacities=[2]
i=1, mask=0, dp_prev=0; weight=3, value=5; m=1 capacities=[2]
i=2, mask=1, dp_prev=10; weight=2, value=20; m=1 capacities=[2]
i=1, mask=0, dp_prev=0; weight=2, value=3; m=2 capacities=[3,3]
i=2, mask=1, dp_prev=3; weight=3, value=4; m=2 capacities=[3,3]
i=3, mask=3, dp_prev=7; weight=1, value=1; m=2 capacities=[3,3]
i=1, mask=0, dp_prev=0; weight=1, value=1; m=3 capacities=[1,1,1]
i=2, mask=1, dp_prev=1; weight=1, value=2; m=3 capacities=[1,1,1]
i=1, mask=0, dp_prev=0; weight=5, value=10; m=2 capacities=[10,10]
i=2, mask=3, dp_prev=20; weight=5, value=10; m=2 capacities=[10,10]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Knapsack Usage
ladders.desc: Using bitmasks to compactly represent which knapsacks are currently used in DP states.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is to represent the usage status of m knapsacks as a bitmask (integer) where each bit corresponds to a knapsack: 0 means unused, 1 means used. This reduces state space from exponential in n to exponential in m (which is small). For example, with m=3, mask 5 (binary 101) means knapsack 0 and 2 are used, knapsack 1 is free. The intuition is that instead of tracking which items are in which knapsack (which would be O(m^n)), we only care about which knapsacks are occupied, as items are processed sequentially. This works because DP progresses item by item, and at each step we decide assignment based on current mask. The mask allows efficient checking (via bit operations) if a knapsack is available and updating when used. Time/space for mask operations is O(1) per check/update. This connects to L1 as the foundation for state transitions. Common mistakes include incorrect bit indexing (0-based vs 1-based) or misunderstanding mask values (e.g., mask 0 means all knapsacks free). Real code pattern: mask ranges from 0 to (1<<m)-1; check bit k: (mask>>k)&1; set bit k: mask|(1<<k).

examples.output: Knapsack usage: knap0 used, knap1 free, knap2 used
examples.trace: Mask 5 in binary: 101. Bit positions: 0 (LSB) → 1 (used), 1 → 0 (free), 2 → 1 (used). So knapsack indices: 0 used, 1 free, 2 used.

examples.input: m=2, update mask=1 (binary 01) by using knapsack 1
examples.trace: Initial mask=01. To use knapsack 1 (index 1), set bit 1: 01 | (1<<1) = 01 | 10 = 11 (decimal 3).

testCases.expected: knap0 free, knap1 used, knap2 used

testCases.input: m=3, update mask=2 (010) by using knap0
m=3, check if knap1 used in mask=5 (101)

testCases.expected: knap0 free, knap1 used, knap2 free, knap3 used

ladders.title: Building Block — 0/1 Knapsack DP with Single Knapsack
ladders.desc: Dynamic programming solution for the classic 0/1 knapsack problem with one knapsack.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational knapsack DP: given n items with weights and values, and a single knapsack capacity C, maximize total value without exceeding weight. We use DP where dp[i][w] = maximum value using first i items with total weight ≤ w. Recurrence: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]) if weight[i] ≤ w, else dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]. Base: dp[0][w]=0. The intuition is to consider each item and decide to take or not take, building up solutions incrementally. Time O(n*C), space O(n*C) or optimized to O(C). This connects to L2 as a simpler case (m=1) where bitmask isn't needed (mask is either 0 or 1). Common mistakes include off-by-one in indices or missing the not-take case. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(C+1); for i in range(n): for w in range(C, weight[i]-1, -1): dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight[i]] + value[i]).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bit Manipulation for Mask Updates
ladders.desc: Fundamental bit operations to check and set bits in an integer mask.
ladders.explanation: This level covers basic bit manipulation: representing a mask as an integer where each bit corresponds to a knapsack status. Key operations: 1) Check if bit k is set: (mask >> k) & 1 returns 1 if set, 0 otherwise. 2) Set bit k: mask | (1 << k) creates a new mask with bit k set. 3) Iterate over all masks from 0 to (1<<m)-1. The intuition is that integers in binary naturally encode subsets of m elements, allowing efficient subset operations. Time O(1) per operation, space O(1). This connects to L3 as the tool for handling multiple knapsacks in DP. Common mistakes include shifting wrong direction or using 1-indexing. Real code pattern: for mask in range(1<<m): # iterate all masks; if (mask>>k) & 1: # bit k is set; new_mask = mask | (1<<k) # set bit k.

examples.trace: Initial mask=1 (01). To set bit1: 1 << 1 = 2 (10). mask | 2 = 1 | 2 = 3 (11).

testCases.input: m=4, mask=5, check bit0 and bit2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Bits and Binary Numbers
ladders.desc: Introduction to binary representation and basic bit concepts for complete beginners.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers binary numbers: how integers are represented in base-2 using bits (0 or 1). For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1). Key concepts: 1) Bit positions: rightmost is least significant bit (LSB, position 0), leftmost is most significant. 2) Bit values: position k has value 2^k. 3) Binary to decimal conversion: sum of bit*2^position. 4) Decimal to binary: repeated division by 2. The intuition is that binary allows compact representation of yes/no states, like knapsack usage. Time/space for basic operations is O(1). This connects to L4 as prerequisite for bit manipulation. Common mistakes include confusing bit positions or miscomputing values. Real code pattern: in Python, bin(5) gives '0b101'; 1<<3 gives 8 (binary 1000).

examples.trace: 5 divided by 2: quotient 2 remainder 1 (LSB). 2/2: quotient 1 remainder 0. 1/2: quotient 0 remainder 1 (MSB). Remainders read backward: 101.
Binary 110: bit positions from right: 0→0, 1→1, 2→1. Value = 0*2^0 + 1*2^1 + 1*2^2 = 0 + 2 + 4 = 6.

approaches.name: Greedy with DP Optimization O(n log n + n * C)
approaches.summary: Sort items by value-to-weight ratio and use DP to pack them into knapsacks, applying greedy heuristics to prune unpromising states. This hybrid approach balances efficiency by reducing the search space while ensuring near-optimal solutions under certain constraints.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n + n * C) where C is total capacity
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n + C) for sorting and DP arrays

ladders.title: Full Problem — Multiple Knapsack with Greedy-DP Hybrid
ladders.desc: Complete solution using greedy sorting and DP optimization for multiple knapsacks
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the multiple knapsack problem by combining greedy heuristics with dynamic programming. The intuition is that while the multiple knapsack problem is NP-hard, we can achieve near-optimal solutions efficiently by first sorting items by value-to-weight ratio (greedy heuristic) to prioritize high-value items, then using DP to pack them optimally within capacity constraints. The greedy sorting reduces the search space dramatically, while DP ensures we don't miss better combinations that might be overlooked by pure greedy. The algorithm works by: 1) Sorting all items in descending order of value/weight ratio. 2) For each knapsack, maintaining a DP array where dp[c] = maximum value achievable with capacity c. 3) Processing items in sorted order, updating DP arrays for all knapsacks. 4) Applying pruning: if an item's value is less than the minimum improvement needed based on remaining capacity, skip it. This hybrid approach achieves O(n log n + n*C) time where n is items and C is total capacity, and O(n + C) space. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the key subroutine of DP state management with the greedy preprocessing. Common mistakes include: not properly handling multiple knapsacks (treating them as one big knapsack), forgetting to sort by value/weight ratio, and incorrect DP state transitions. Real code pattern: def multiple_knapsack(items, capacities): items.sort(key=lambda x: x.value/x.weight, reverse=True); total_cap = sum(capacities); dp = [0]*(total_cap+1); for item in items: for c in range(total_cap, item.weight-1, -1): dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-item.weight] + item.value); return max(dp[:min(total_cap, sum(c.weight for c in capacities))+1])

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort by ratio: 40/5=8.0, 50/10=5.0, 70/15≈4.67 → order: item1(40,5), item2(50,10), item3(70,15). Step 2: Total capacity=20, dp[0..20]=0. Step 3: Process item1: for c=20 to 5: dp[5]=40, dp[10]=40, dp[15]=40, dp[20]=40. Step 4: Process item2: for c=20 to 10: dp[10]=max(40,0+50)=50, dp[15]=max(40,40+50)=90, dp[20]=max(40,40+50)=90. Step 5: Process item3: for c=20 to 15: dp[15]=max(90,40+70)=110 (but weight 15 exceeds individual knapsack cap 10, so invalid), dp[20]=max(90,50+70)=120 (invalid for same reason). Step 6: Adjust for multiple knapsacks: we need to ensure no knapsack exceeds its capacity. With capacities [10,10], max valid dp is 90 (item1+item2 across two knapsacks).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP State Management for Knapsack
ladders.desc: Dynamic programming to track maximum value for each capacity
ladders.explanation: This subroutine implements the core DP logic for the knapsack problem. The intuition is that we can build up the solution incrementally by considering items one by one and maintaining an array dp where dp[c] represents the maximum value achievable with capacity c using items processed so far. This works because of the optimal substructure property: the optimal solution for capacity c either excludes the current item (dp[c] remains unchanged) or includes it (dp[c-weight] + value). By processing items in a specific order and updating dp from high to low capacities, we ensure each item is used at most once (0/1 knapsack). The algorithm: 1) Initialize dp array of size (total_capacity+1) with zeros. 2) For each item (value, weight): iterate c from total_capacity down to weight: dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-weight] + value). 3) The answer is max(dp). Time complexity is O(n*C) where n is items and C is total capacity, space is O(C). This feeds into L0 by providing the optimization mechanism after greedy sorting. Common mistakes include: updating dp from low to high (which allows unlimited items), not initializing dp[0]=0, and incorrect array bounds. Real code pattern: def knapsack_dp(items, capacity): dp = [0]*(capacity+1); for v,w in items: for c in range(capacity, w-1, -1): dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-w] + v); return max(dp).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Sorting by Value-to-Weight Ratio
ladders.desc: Sort items to prioritize high value per unit weight
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves sorting all items in descending order of their value-to-weight ratio (value/weight). The intuition is that items with higher ratio provide more value per unit of capacity, so considering them first increases the likelihood of achieving a high total value efficiently. This greedy heuristic doesn't guarantee optimality for 0/1 knapsack but works well in practice, especially when combined with DP. It reduces the search space by eliminating obviously poor items early. The algorithm: 1) For each item, compute ratio = value/weight. 2) Sort items by ratio in descending order. 3) Process items in this sorted order. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting, space is O(n) for storing sorted items. This feeds into L1 by providing the input order for DP processing. Common mistakes include: using integer division (should use float), not handling zero weight (add check), and forgetting to sort descending. Real code pattern: def sort_by_ratio(items): return sorted(items, key=lambda x: x[0]/x[1], reverse=True).

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Capacities in Reverse
ladders.desc: Processing DP array from high to low to prevent item reuse
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating over capacity values in descending order when updating the DP array. The intuition is that for 0/1 knapsack (each item used at most once), if we update from high capacity to low, we ensure that when considering dp[c] for a current item, dp[c-weight] hasn't been updated yet in this iteration, so it represents the best value without including the current item. If we updated from low to high, dp[c-weight] might already include the current item, leading to using it multiple times. The algorithm: given item with weight w, loop c from max_capacity down to w: update dp[c]. This ensures each item is considered exactly once per capacity evaluation. Time complexity per item is O(C), space is O(1) extra. This feeds into L2 by being a critical step in the DP subroutine. Common mistakes include: incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one), using ascending order, and not checking c >= w. Real code pattern: for c in range(capacity, weight-1, -1): dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-weight] + value).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial dp=[0,0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process item value=3, weight=2. Step 3: Loop c=4 to 2: c=4: dp[4]=max(0, dp[2]+3)=max(0,0+3)=3; c=3: dp[3]=max(0, dp[1]+3)=max(0,0+3)=3; c=2: dp[2]=max(0, dp[0]+3)=max(0,0+3)=3. Step 4: Final dp=[0,0,3,3,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Updating Maximum Values
ladders.desc: Using max() to choose between including or excluding an item
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two values and updating a variable to the maximum. In the knapsack DP, for each capacity c, we compare the current best value (excluding the current item) with the value if we include the current item (value + best value at capacity c-weight). The intuition is that we want to maximize total value, so at each step we choose the better option. The algorithm: new_value = max(old_value, dp[c-weight] + item_value). This is applied repeatedly in the DP loop. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison, space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as it's the core update within the reverse iteration. Common mistakes include: incorrect addition (mixing value and weight), not using max(), and off-by-one in indexing. Real code pattern: dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-weight] + value).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute include_value = dp_at_c_weight + item_value = 5 + 8 = 13. Step 2: Compare old_value=10 and include_value=13 using max(10,13). Step 3: Result = 13.
Step 1: include_value = 12 + 6 = 18. Step 2: max(20,18) = 20. Step 3: Result = 20.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Value and Weight Pairs
ladders.desc: Basic representation of items with value and weight attributes
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding that each item in a knapsack problem has two attributes: value (how beneficial it is) and weight (how much capacity it consumes). The intuition is that we want to select items to maximize total value without exceeding capacity constraints. Items are typically represented as pairs (value, weight) or objects with these properties. This is the simplest building block because all knapsack variations build upon this representation. For example, an item with value 10 and weight 3 means it provides 10 units of benefit and takes up 3 units of space. The algorithm: define items as list of tuples [(v1,w1), (v2,w2), ...]. Time and space are O(1) per item. This feeds into L4 by providing the data for max comparisons. Common mistakes include: confusing value and weight order, using negative values, or zero weight without handling. Real code pattern: items = [(10,2), (20,5), (15,3)].

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify value=5. Step 2: Identify weight=2. Step 3: Represent as pair (5,2).
Step 1: Value=12. Step 2: Weight=4. Step 3: Pair is (12,4).
